From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Drugs used to achieve relief from pain

For the medical condition of hereditary analgesia, see [Congenital insensitivity to pain](/wiki/Congenital_insensitivity_to_pain "Congenital insensitivity to pain").

"Painkiller" and similar terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Painkiller (disambiguation)](/wiki/Painkiller_(disambiguation) "Painkiller (disambiguation)").

An **analgesic drug**, also called simply an **analgesic**, **antalgic**, **pain reliever**, or **painkiller**, is any member of the group of [drugs](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug") used for [pain management](/wiki/Pain_management "Pain management"). Analgesics are conceptually distinct from [anesthetics](/wiki/Anesthetic "Anesthetic"), which temporarily reduce, and in some instances eliminate, [sensation](/wiki/Sense "Sense"), although [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesia "Analgesia") and [anesthesia](/wiki/Anesthesia "Anesthesia") are neurophysiologically overlapping and thus various drugs have both analgesic and anesthetic effects.

Analgesic choice is also determined by the type of pain: For [neuropathic pain](/wiki/Neuropathic_pain "Neuropathic pain"), recent research has suggested that classes of drugs that are not normally considered analgesics, such as [tricyclic antidepressants](/wiki/Tricyclic_antidepressants "Tricyclic antidepressants") and [anticonvulsants](/wiki/Anticonvulsant "Anticonvulsant") may be considered as an alternative.[[1]](#cite_note-pmid14623723-1)

Various analgesics, such as many [NSAIDs](/wiki/Nonsteroidal_anti-inflammatory_drug "Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug"), are available [over the counter](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug") in most countries, whereas various others are [prescription drugs](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug") owing to the substantial risks and high chances of [overdose](/wiki/Drug_overdose "Drug overdose"), [misuse](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse"), and [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction") in the absence of medical supervision.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *analgesic* derives from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") *an-* (ἀν-, "without"), *álgos* (ἄλγος, "pain"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) and *-ikos* (-ικος, forming [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective")). Such drugs were usually known as "[anodynes](/wiki/Anodyne "Anodyne")" before the 20th century.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEB1878-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEB1911-4)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

Analgesics are typically classified based on their mechanism of action.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Tylenol.jpg/250px-Tylenol.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tylenol.jpg)

A bottle of [acetaminophen](/wiki/Acetaminophen "Acetaminophen")

### Paracetamol (acetaminophen)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Paracetamol (acetaminophen)")]

Main article: [Paracetamol](/wiki/Paracetamol "Paracetamol")

Paracetamol, also known as acetaminophen or APAP, is a medication used to treat [pain](/wiki/Pain "Pain") and [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever").[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS2016-6) It is typically used for mild to moderate pain.[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS2016-6) In combination with [opioid pain medication](/wiki/Opioid_analgesic "Opioid analgesic"), paracetamol is now used for more severe pain such as [cancer pain](/wiki/Cancer_pain "Cancer pain") and after surgery.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is typically used either by mouth or [rectally](/wiki/Rectally "Rectally") but is also available [intravenously](/wiki/Intravenously "Intravenously").[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS2016-6)[[8]](#cite_note-Hoch2014-8) Effects last between two and four hours.[[8]](#cite_note-Hoch2014-8) Paracetamol is classified as a mild analgesic,[[8]](#cite_note-Hoch2014-8) and is generally safe at recommended doses.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### NSAIDs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: NSAIDs")]

Main article: [Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug](/wiki/Nonsteroidal_anti-inflammatory_drug "Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug")

Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (usually abbreviated to NSAIDs), are a [drug class](/wiki/Drug_class "Drug class") that groups together [drugs](/wiki/Drug "Drug") that decrease pain[[10]](#cite_note-researchgate.net-10) and [lower fever](/wiki/Antipyretic "Antipyretic"), and, in higher doses, decrease [inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation").[[11]](#cite_note-Mallinson-11) The most prominent members of this group of drugs — [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin"), [ibuprofen](/wiki/Ibuprofen "Ibuprofen"), [naproxen](/wiki/Naproxen "Naproxen"), and [diclofenac](/wiki/Diclofenac "Diclofenac") — are all available [over the counter](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug") in most countries.[[12]](#cite_note-The_Physician_and_Sportsmedicine_2010-12)

#### COX-2 inhibitors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: COX-2 inhibitors")]

Main article: [COX-2 inhibitor](/wiki/COX-2_inhibitor "COX-2 inhibitor")

These drugs have been derived from NSAIDs. The [cyclooxygenase](/wiki/Cyclooxygenase "Cyclooxygenase") enzyme inhibited by NSAIDs was discovered to have at least two different versions: COX1 and COX2. Research suggested most of the adverse effects of NSAIDs to be mediated by blocking the COX1 ([constitutive](/wiki/Constitutive_enzyme "Constitutive enzyme")) enzyme, with the analgesic effects being mediated by the COX2 ([inducible](/wiki/Adaptive_enzyme "Adaptive enzyme")) enzyme. Thus, the COX2 inhibitors were developed to inhibit only the COX2 enzyme (traditional NSAIDs block both versions in general). These drugs (such as [rofecoxib](/wiki/Rofecoxib "Rofecoxib"), [celecoxib](/wiki/Celecoxib "Celecoxib"), and [etoricoxib](/wiki/Etoricoxib "Etoricoxib")) are equally effective analgesics when compared with NSAIDs, but cause less gastrointestinal hemorrhage in particular.[[13]](#cite_note-Conaghan2012-13)

After widespread adoption of the COX-2 inhibitors, it was discovered that most of the drugs in this class increase the risk of [cardiovascular events](/wiki/Cardiovascular_event "Cardiovascular event") by 40% on average. This led to the withdrawal of rofecoxib and valdecoxib, and warnings on others. Etoricoxib seems relatively safe, with the risk of [thrombotic](/wiki/Thrombotic "Thrombotic") events similar to that of non-coxib NSAID diclofenac.[[13]](#cite_note-Conaghan2012-13)

### Opioids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Opioids")]

Main article: [Opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid")

[Morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), the archetypal [opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid"), and other opioids (e.g., [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone"), [hydrocodone](/wiki/Hydrocodone "Hydrocodone"), [dihydromorphine](/wiki/Dihydromorphine "Dihydromorphine"), [pethidine](/wiki/Pethidine "Pethidine")) all exert a similar influence on the [cerebral](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum") [opioid receptor](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor") system. [Buprenorphine](/wiki/Buprenorphine "Buprenorphine") is a [partial agonist](/wiki/Partial_agonist "Partial agonist") of the μ-opioid receptor, and [tramadol](/wiki/Tramadol "Tramadol") is a serotonin norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) with weak μ-opioid receptor agonist properties.[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Tramadol](/wiki/Tramadol "Tramadol") is structurally closer to [venlafaxine](/wiki/Venlafaxine "Venlafaxine") than to [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") and delivers analgesia by not only delivering "opioid-like" effects (through mild agonism of the [mu receptor](/wiki/Mu_receptor "Mu receptor")) but also by acting as a weak but fast-acting [serotonin releasing agent](/wiki/Serotonin_releasing_agent "Serotonin releasing agent") and [norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor](/wiki/Norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor").[[15]](#cite_note-pmid1596676-15)[[16]](#cite_note-pmid9389855-16)[[17]](#cite_note-pmid9671098-17)[[18]](#cite_note-pmid12354291-18) [Tapentadol](/wiki/Tapentadol "Tapentadol"), with some structural similarities to tramadol, presents what is believed to be a novel drug working through two (and possibly three) different modes of action in the fashion of both a traditional opioid and as an SNRI. The effects of serotonin and [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor") on pain, while not completely understood, have had causal links established and drugs in the SNRI class are commonly used in conjunction with opioids (especially tapentadol and tramadol) with greater success in pain relief.

Dosing of all opioids may be limited by opioid toxicity (confusion, respiratory depression, [myoclonic jerks](/wiki/Myoclonus "Myoclonus") and pinpoint pupils), seizures ([tramadol](/wiki/Tramadol "Tramadol")), but opioid-tolerant individuals usually have higher dose ceilings than patients without tolerance.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
Opioids, while very effective analgesics, may have some unpleasant side-effects. Patients starting morphine may experience [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea") and [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting") (generally relieved by a short course of [antiemetics](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") such as [phenergan](/wiki/Phenergan "Phenergan")). [Pruritus](/wiki/Pruritus "Pruritus") (itching) may require switching to a different opioid. [Constipation](/wiki/Constipation "Constipation") occurs in almost all patients on opioids, and [laxatives](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative") ([lactulose](/wiki/Lactulose "Lactulose"), [macrogol](/wiki/Macrogol "Macrogol")-containing or co-danthramer) are typically co-prescribed.[[20]](#cite_note-oxford-20)

When used appropriately, opioids and other central analgesics are safe and effective; however, risks such as addiction and the body's becoming used to the drug (tolerance) can occur. The effect of tolerance means that frequent use of the drug may result in its diminished effect. When safe to do so, the dosage may need to be increased to maintain effectiveness against tolerance, which may be of particular concern regarding patients with chronic pain and requiring an analgesic over long periods. Opioid tolerance is often addressed with [opioid rotation therapy](/wiki/Opioid_rotation "Opioid rotation") in which a patient is routinely switched between two or more non-cross-tolerant opioid medications in order to prevent exceeding safe dosages in the attempt to achieve an adequate analgesic effect.

Opioid tolerance should not be confused with [opioid-induced hyperalgesia](/wiki/Opioid-induced_hyperalgesia "Opioid-induced hyperalgesia"). The symptoms of these two conditions can appear very similar but the mechanism of action is different. Opioid-induced hyperalgesia is when exposure to opioids increases the sensation of pain ([hyperalgesia](/wiki/Hyperalgesia "Hyperalgesia")) and can even make non-painful stimuli painful ([allodynia](/wiki/Allodynia "Allodynia")).[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Alcohol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Alcohol")]

See also: [Ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol")

Alcohol has biological, mental, and social effects which influence the consequences of using alcohol for pain.[[22]](#cite_note-ZaleMaisto2015-22) Moderate use of alcohol can lessen certain types of pain in certain circumstances.[[22]](#cite_note-ZaleMaisto2015-22)

The majority of its analgesic effects come from antagonizing NMDA receptors, similarly to ketamine, thus decreasing the activity of the primary excitatory (signal boosting) [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter"), glutamate. It also functions as an analgesic to a lesser degree by increasing the activity of the primary inhibitory (signal reducing) neurotransmitter, GABA.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Attempting to use alcohol to treat pain has also been observed to lead to negative outcomes including excessive drinking and [alcohol use disorder](/wiki/Alcohol_use_disorder "Alcohol use disorder").[[22]](#cite_note-ZaleMaisto2015-22)

### Cannabis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cannabis")]

Main article: [Medical cannabis](/wiki/Medical_cannabis "Medical cannabis")

*Medical cannabis*, or *medical marijuana*, refers to [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") or its [cannabinoids](/wiki/Cannabinoids "Cannabinoids") used to treat disease or improve symptoms.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) There is evidence suggesting that cannabis can be used to treat [chronic pain](/wiki/Chronic_pain "Chronic pain") and [muscle spasms](/wiki/Muscle_spasms "Muscle spasms"), with some trials indicating improved relief of neuropathic pain over opioids.[[26]](#cite_note-Borgelt2013-26)[[27]](#cite_note-JAMA2015-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Combinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Combinations")]

Analgesics are frequently used in combination, such as the [paracetamol](/wiki/Paracetamol "Paracetamol") and [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") preparations found in many non-prescription pain relievers. They can also be found in combination with vasoconstrictor drugs such as [pseudoephedrine](/wiki/Pseudoephedrine "Pseudoephedrine") for [sinus](/wiki/Paranasal_sinus "Paranasal sinus")-related preparations, or with [antihistamine](/wiki/Antihistamine "Antihistamine") drugs for people with allergies.

While the use of paracetamol, aspirin, [ibuprofen](/wiki/Ibuprofen "Ibuprofen"), [naproxen](/wiki/Naproxen "Naproxen"), and other [NSAIDS](/wiki/NSAIDS "NSAIDS") concurrently with weak to mid-range opiates (up to about the hydrocodone level) has been said to show beneficial synergistic effects by combating pain at multiple sites of action,[[29]](#cite_note-pmid34636261-29)[[30]](#cite_note-pmid12148679-30) several combination analgesic products have been shown to have few efficacy benefits when compared to similar doses of their individual components. Moreover, these combination analgesics can often result in significant adverse events, including accidental overdoses, most often due to confusion that arises from the multiple (and often non-acting) components of these combinations.[[31]](#cite_note-nps01-31)

### Alternative medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Alternative medicine")]

There is some evidence that some treatments using alternative medicine can relieve some types of pain more effectively than [placebo](/wiki/Placebo "Placebo").[[32]](#cite_note-alternative_medicine_reviews-32) The available research concludes that more research would be necessary to better understand the use of alternative medicine.[[32]](#cite_note-alternative_medicine_reviews-32)

### Other drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other drugs")]

[Nefopam](/wiki/Nefopam "Nefopam")—a monoamine reuptake inhibitor, and calcium and sodium channel modulator—is also approved for the treatment of moderate to severe pain in some countries.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[Flupirtine](/wiki/Flupirtine "Flupirtine") is a centrally acting K+ channel opener with weak [NMDA antagonist](/wiki/NMDA_antagonist "NMDA antagonist") properties.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It was used in Europe for moderate to strong pain, as well as its [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine")-treating and muscle-relaxant properties. It has no significant [anticholinergic](/wiki/Anticholinergic "Anticholinergic") properties, and is believed to be devoid of any activity on dopamine, serotonin, or histamine receptors. It is not addictive, and tolerance usually does not develop.[[35]](#cite_note-35) However, tolerance may develop in some cases.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[Ziconotide](/wiki/Ziconotide "Ziconotide"), a blocker of potent [N-type voltage-gated calcium channels](/wiki/N-type_calcium_channel "N-type calcium channel"), is administered [intrathecally](/wiki/Intrathecally "Intrathecally") for the relief of severe, usually cancer-related pain.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Adjuvants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Adjuvants")]

Main article: [Analgesic adjuvant](/wiki/Analgesic_adjuvant "Analgesic adjuvant")

Certain drugs that have been introduced for uses other than analgesics are also used in pain management. Both first-generation (such as [amitriptyline](/wiki/Amitriptyline "Amitriptyline")) and newer [antidepressants](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor") (such as [duloxetine](/wiki/Duloxetine "Duloxetine")) are used alongside NSAIDs and opioids for pain involving nerve damage and similar problems.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Other agents directly potentiate the effects of analgesics, such as using [hydroxyzine](/wiki/Hydroxyzine "Hydroxyzine"), [promethazine](/wiki/Promethazine "Promethazine"), [carisoprodol](/wiki/Carisoprodol "Carisoprodol"), or [tripelennamine](/wiki/Tripelennamine "Tripelennamine") to increase the pain-killing ability of a given dose of opioid analgesic.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Adjuvant analgesics, also called atypical analgesics, include [orphenadrine](/wiki/Orphenadrine "Orphenadrine"), [mexiletine](/wiki/Mexiletine "Mexiletine"), [pregabalin](/wiki/Pregabalin "Pregabalin"), [gabapentin](/wiki/Gabapentin "Gabapentin"), [cyclobenzaprine](/wiki/Cyclobenzaprine "Cyclobenzaprine"), [hyoscine](/wiki/Hyoscine "Hyoscine") (scopolamine), and other drugs possessing anticonvulsant, anticholinergic, and/or antispasmodic properties, as well as many other drugs with CNS actions. These drugs are used along with analgesics to modulate and/or modify the action of opioids when used against pain, especially of neuropathic origin.

[Dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan") has been noted to slow the development of and reverse tolerance to opioids, as well as to exert additional analgesia by acting upon [NMDA](/wiki/NMDA "NMDA") receptors, as does [ketamine](/wiki/Ketamine "Ketamine").[[41]](#cite_note-41) Some analgesics such as [methadone](/wiki/Methadone "Methadone") and [ketobemidone](/wiki/Ketobemidone "Ketobemidone") and perhaps [piritramide](/wiki/Piritramide "Piritramide") have intrinsic NMDA action.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The [anticonvulsant](/wiki/Anticonvulsant "Anticonvulsant") [carbamazepine](/wiki/Carbamazepine "Carbamazepine") is used to treat neuropathic pain. Similarly, the [gabapentinoids](/wiki/Gabapentinoid "Gabapentinoid") [gabapentin](/wiki/Gabapentin "Gabapentin") and [pregabalin](/wiki/Pregabalin "Pregabalin") are prescribed for neuropathic pain, and [phenibut](/wiki/Phenibut "Phenibut") is available without prescription. Gabapentinoids work as α2δ-subunit blockers of [voltage-gated calcium channels](/wiki/Voltage-gated_calcium_channel "Voltage-gated calcium channel"), and tend to have other mechanisms of action as well. Gabapentinoids are all [anticonvulsants](/wiki/Anticonvulsant "Anticonvulsant"), which are most commonly used for neuropathic pain, as their mechanism of action tends to inhibit pain sensation originating from the nervous system.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Other uses")]

Topical analgesia is generally recommended to avoid systemic side-effects. Painful joints, for example, may be treated with an [ibuprofen](/wiki/Ibuprofen "Ibuprofen")- or [diclofenac](/wiki/Diclofenac "Diclofenac")-containing gel (The labeling for topical diclofenac has been updated to warn about drug-induced hepatotoxicity.[[44]](#cite_note-44)); [capsaicin](/wiki/Capsaicin "Capsaicin") also is used [topically](/wiki/Topical_cream "Topical cream"). [Lidocaine](/wiki/Lidocaine "Lidocaine"), an [anesthetic](/wiki/Anesthetic "Anesthetic"), and [steroids](/wiki/Glucocorticoid "Glucocorticoid") may be injected into joints for longer-term pain relief. Lidocaine is also used for painful [mouth sores](/wiki/Mouth_sore "Mouth sore") and to numb areas for [dental](/wiki/Dentistry "Dentistry") work and minor medical procedures. In February 2007 the FDA notified consumers and healthcare professionals of the potential hazards of topical anesthetics entering the bloodstream when applied in large doses to the skin without medical supervision. These topical anesthetics contain anesthetic drugs such as lidocaine, tetracaine, benzocaine, and prilocaine in a cream, ointment, or gel.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Uses")]

Topical [nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs](/wiki/Nonsteroidal_anti-inflammatory_drugs "Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs") provide pain relief in common conditions such as muscle sprains and overuse injuries. Since the side effects are also lesser, topical preparations could be preferred over oral medications in these conditions.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Analgesic&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Research")]

See also: [List of investigational analgesics](/wiki/List_of_investigational_analgesics "List of investigational analgesics")

Some novel and investigational analgesics include subtype-selective [voltage-gated sodium channel](/wiki/Voltage-gated_sodium_channel "Voltage-gated sodium channel") [blockers](/wiki/Sodium_channel_blocker "Sodium channel blocker") such as [funapide](/wiki/Funapide "Funapide") and [raxatrigine](/wiki/Raxatrigine "Raxatrigine"), as well as multimodal agents such as [ralfinamide](/wiki/Ralfinamide "Ralfinamide").[[131]](#cite_note-131)
